promptText: Your people have settled new land, but shelter is scarce. Where will you focus your first construction efforts?

options.label: Build a Farm
options.description: Secure food production for your growing population

options.label: Build a Granary
options.description: Protect existing food stores from spoilage

options.label: Build Walls
options.description: Defense first — protect what you have

historicalContext: Early civilizations in Mesopotamia and the Indus Valley faced the same choice: invest in agriculture to feed more people, or fortify against raiders. The Sumerians chose irrigation canals first, which led to food surpluses that funded everything else.
scaffolding6th: As the Architect, I propose building [choice] because in early civilizations like [place], we learned that [fact]. This will help our civilization by [outcome].
scaffolding78: What infrastructure does your civilization need most urgently? What historical example supports prioritizing this type of building? What are the risks of delaying other construction?

promptText: Your river-fed lands are productive but vulnerable to drought. An ambitious water project has been proposed.

options.label: Build an Aqueduct
options.description: Permanent drought immunity for this sub-zone

options.label: Expand Farms instead
options.description: More food now, deal with drought later

options.label: Build a Market
options.description: Trade for water resources rather than build

historicalContext: Roman aqueducts like the Pont du Gard carried water over 50 kilometers. The investment was enormous, but cities that had them thrived while others withered during dry seasons.
scaffolding6th: As the Architect, I want to [choice] because the Romans showed us that [fact]. Our civilization will benefit because [outcome].
scaffolding78: What is the long-term vs. short-term trade-off here? How did historical civilizations handle water infrastructure? What happens to your population if drought hits without preparation?

promptText: Reports of hostile armies have reached your council. Your borders are exposed.

options.label: Build Barracks
options.description: Reduce soldier costs and train troops faster

options.label: Reinforce Walls
options.description: First attack each epoch absorbed at zero loss

options.label: Build a Library
options.description: Invest in knowledge — tech advantage over brute force

historicalContext: The Great Wall of China and Hadrian's Wall in Britain were massive defensive investments. But the Mongols proved that no wall can stop a determined, innovative enemy. Some civilizations chose technological superiority instead.
scaffolding6th: As the Architect, I choose to [choice] because history shows that [fact]. This protects our civilization by [outcome].
scaffolding78: Is defense through walls or through military strength more effective historically? What examples support your reasoning? What happens if your enemy has technology you don't?

promptText: Scouts report rich lands beyond your borders. But expanding costs resources. How will you grow?

options.label: Send Scouts to explore
options.description: Reveal 3 hidden sub-zones through fog of war

options.label: Claim adjacent territory
options.description: Expand your borders to resource-rich land

options.label: Establish a trade route
options.description: Let merchants bring wealth without territorial risk

historicalContext: The Phoenicians expanded through trade networks spanning the Mediterranean, while Rome expanded through military conquest. Both became powerful, but through very different paths with different costs.
scaffolding6th: As the Merchant, I propose [choice] because civilizations like [name] showed that [fact]. This will grow our reach by [outcome].
scaffolding78: What is the risk-reward calculus of exploration vs. trade vs. territorial expansion? Which historical civilizations grew most successfully through each method?

promptText: A neighboring civilization has resources you need. They've signaled willingness to negotiate.

options.label: Propose a Trade Agreement
options.description: Steady resource exchange over multiple epochs

options.label: Offer a Spot Trade
options.description: One-time exchange of surplus for what you need

options.label: Impose an Embargo
options.description: Cut them off from your markets as leverage

historicalContext: The Silk Road created centuries of prosperity through sustained trade agreements. But Venice's trade embargoes against rivals showed that economic warfare could be as devastating as military conflict.
scaffolding6th: As the Merchant, I want to [choice] because the [Silk Road/Venice example] teaches us that [fact]. Our economy will [outcome].
scaffolding78: What are the diplomatic implications of each trade approach? How did historical trade networks like the Silk Road affect civilizations that participated vs. those excluded?

promptText: Your civilization needs its first law. What principle will define your people?

options.label: Law of Hospitality
options.description: +Reach bonus, foreigners welcomed

options.label: Law of the Land
options.description: +Food bonus, agricultural rights established

options.label: Law of Arms
options.description: +Resilience bonus, militia required

options.label: Law of Knowledge
options.description: +Legacy bonus, education mandated

historicalContext: Hammurabi's Code in Babylon was one of the first written law systems. The choice of laws shaped civilizations for millennia — Athens chose democratic laws that prioritized individual rights, while Sparta chose military laws that prioritized collective defense.
scaffolding6th: As the Diplomat, I propose the [law name] because in [historical civilization], their laws about [topic] led to [outcome]. This will define our people as [identity].
scaffolding78: How do founding laws shape a civilization's identity and trajectory? What historical examples show the long-term consequences of different legal priorities?

promptText: Tensions are rising with a neighboring civilization. War seemed inevitable — until their diplomat sent a message requesting talks.

options.label: Accept peace talks
options.description: Negotiate terms to avoid war

options.label: Demand tribute first
options.description: Show strength before negotiating

options.label: Reject and prepare for war
options.description: Some conflicts can only be settled by force

historicalContext: The Peace of Westphalia (1648) ended decades of European war through diplomacy. But the Munich Agreement (1938) showed that appeasement can also fail catastrophically. Knowing when to fight and when to talk is one of history's hardest lessons.
scaffolding6th: As the Diplomat, I believe we should [choice] because in history, [example] showed that [fact]. This decision will affect our civilization by [outcome].
scaffolding78: When is diplomacy more effective than military action, and vice versa? What historical examples support your position? What are the risks of each path?

promptText: Raiders have been spotted near your borders. Your military advisor awaits your orders.

options.label: Train new soldiers
options.description: Strengthen your standing army

options.label: Fortify borders
options.description: Build defensive positions in vulnerable sub-zones

options.label: Send a diplomatic warning
options.description: Show of force without committing troops

historicalContext: The Roman legions maintained standing armies at enormous cost, but it gave them rapid response capability. The Chinese invested in the Great Wall instead. Both strategies had strengths and vulnerabilities.
scaffolding6th: As the Warlord, I order [choice] because [civilization] showed that [fact]. This will protect our people by [outcome].
scaffolding78: What is the strategic difference between offensive and defensive military postures? How did different civilizations balance military readiness with economic cost?

promptText: Your armies are weary. War exhaustion grips the land. But threats remain at your borders.

options.label: Rest and recover
options.description: Pull back troops, reduce exhaustion

options.label: Hire mercenaries
options.description: Costly but avoids further exhaustion to your people

options.label: Seek alliance
options.description: Share the defense burden with a neighbor

historicalContext: After the Peloponnesian War, Athens was so exhausted that it never recovered its former glory. Rome after the Third Century Crisis nearly collapsed from military overextension. Sometimes the bravest decision is knowing when to stop fighting.
scaffolding6th: As the Warlord, I recommend [choice] because after wars like [example], civilizations that [action] were able to [outcome].
scaffolding78: How does war exhaustion compound over time? What historical examples show civilizations that recovered from military exhaustion vs. those that didn't?

promptText: Your people gather around the fire. They ask: What creature guards our civilization?

options.label: A great eagle
options.description: Symbol of vision and freedom — +Reach

options.label: A mighty bear
options.description: Symbol of strength and protection — +Resilience

options.label: A wise serpent
options.description: Symbol of knowledge and cunning — +Legacy

options.label: A tireless ox
options.description: Symbol of labor and abundance — +Production

historicalContext: Every civilization created mythology. The Romans had the she-wolf, Egypt had the sphinx, Aztecs had the feathered serpent Quetzalcoatl. These symbols became the identity of their peoples and influenced their values for centuries.
scaffolding6th: As the Lorekeeper, I choose [creature] as our guardian because in [civilization], their symbol [name] represented [value]. Our creature will inspire our people to [outcome].
scaffolding78: How do mythological figures reflect a civilization's values? What does your choice say about what your civilization prioritizes? How might this creature appear in future cultural artifacts?

promptText: Generations of your people have lived and died. How will your civilization preserve their stories?

options.label: Create an oral tradition
options.description: Storytellers carry knowledge — +Cultural Influence

options.label: Invent a writing system
options.description: Record knowledge permanently — +Legacy each epoch

options.label: Build a monument
options.description: Stone record that cannot be forgotten — +CI spread

historicalContext: The Sumerians invented cuneiform to record trade, then discovered it also preserved stories. Ancient Griots in West Africa preserved centuries of history orally. The Egyptians carved records in stone. Each method shaped what survived to be remembered.
scaffolding6th: As the Lorekeeper, I believe we should [choice] because [civilization] showed that [fact]. Our civilization's history will be preserved through [outcome].
scaffolding78: What are the tradeoffs between oral, written, and monumental memory? What has been lost or preserved by each method? Which would best serve your civilization right now?

promptText: A neighboring civilization has very different beliefs and stories. Their culture is spreading into your territory.

options.label: Embrace cultural exchange
options.description: Adopt some of their stories — CI from both civilizations

options.label: Strengthen your own mythology
options.description: Double down on your unique identity — +CI spread

options.label: Blend the cultures strategically
options.description: Create a new synthesis — +Legacy and +CI

historicalContext: When Rome conquered Greece, Rome adopted much of Greek culture — Greek gods became Roman gods. Japan blended Buddhism from China with their native Shinto beliefs. Cultural exchange can strengthen or dilute identity, depending on how it is managed.
scaffolding6th: As the Lorekeeper, I recommend [choice] because when [civilization] met [civilization], they [action] and it led to [outcome]. Our culture will [benefit/risk].
scaffolding78: When cultures come into contact, what determines which culture dominates, blends, or disappears? What historical examples illustrate these outcomes?

promptText: Your civilization needs infrastructure to move goods and people. Where will the Merchant invest?

options.label: Build a Market
options.description: Trade post in your best sub-zone — generates Reach income

options.label: Construct a Road
options.description: Connect sub-zones — all movement and delivery faster

options.label: Establish a Harbor
options.description: Coastal trade access — unlocks naval routes (coastal terrain only)

historicalContext: Rome's roads connected 250,000 miles of empire, enabling both trade and military movement. The Grand Canal in China linked northern and southern economies. Infrastructure investments paid dividends for centuries — but required enormous upfront commitment.
scaffolding6th: As the Merchant, I propose building [choice] because [civilization] showed that [fact]. This will strengthen our economy by [outcome].
scaffolding78: What type of trade infrastructure produces the biggest long-term return? How did the Roman road network or Chinese canal systems transform their economies?

promptText: Contact with neighboring civilizations has opened new economic possibilities. How will you build for trade?

options.label: Build a Trading Post at the border
options.description: Dedicated exchange point — +Reach, reduces trade friction

options.label: Invest in a Caravan Route
options.description: Regular supply line — steady income over multiple epochs

options.label: Construct a Lending House
options.description: Finance larger deals — once per epoch, double a trade's value

historicalContext: Medieval trading cities like Venice and Genoa grew wealthy by building infrastructure specifically for international commerce. The Han Dynasty's investment in Silk Road waypoints turned a dangerous journey into a reliable trade corridor.
scaffolding6th: As the Merchant, I choose to [choice] because traders in [civilization] discovered that [fact]. Our economy will benefit because [outcome].
scaffolding78: How does trade infrastructure differ from military or agricultural infrastructure in its economic effects? What made the Silk Road sustainable for centuries?

promptText: Your treasury is full. A prosperous civilization attracts both merchants — and thieves. How do you protect your wealth?

options.label: Build a Vault and guard system
options.description: Secure resources against raids — bank decay reduced

options.label: Invest in a Trade Fleet
options.description: Diversify income across regions — three new trade routes

options.label: Found a Merchant Guild
options.description: Permanent institution — Merchants generate +2 Reach each epoch

historicalContext: The Medici banking family in Florence created the first international banking network, protecting and multiplying their wealth across Europe. The Dutch East India Company showed how formal trade institutions could generate wealth far beyond any individual merchant.
scaffolding6th: As the Merchant, I recommend [choice] because wealthy civilizations like [example] learned that [fact]. This will protect and grow our prosperity by [outcome].
scaffolding78: What distinguishes individual wealth from institutional wealth? How do formal trade institutions change a civilization's economic stability?

promptText: Your civilization has grown too large for the elders alone to govern. A new structure is needed. What will you build?

options.label: A Council Hall
options.description: Democratic decision-making — +Reach, slower decisions

options.label: A Palace District
options.description: Centralized authority — +Production efficiency, less flexibility

options.label: A Court of Law
options.description: Rule of law over rule of person — stable governance, +Legacy

historicalContext: Athens built the first direct democracy with its Assembly Hall. Rome's Senate balanced patrician power. The Zhou Dynasty in China built a palace-centered administration. Each structure created a different kind of civilization with different strengths.
scaffolding6th: As the Diplomat, I propose building [choice] because in [civilization], their form of government [fact]. Our civilization will be shaped because [outcome].
scaffolding78: How does governmental architecture reflect political values? What are the long-term tradeoffs between centralized and distributed decision-making?

promptText: Neighboring civilizations are watching your every move. Your infrastructure sends a message about who you are. What will you build?

options.label: An Embassy District
options.description: Formal welcome to foreign envoys — +contact range, -hostility

options.label: A Watchtower Network
options.description: Early warning of threats — intelligence on neighbor movements

options.label: A Public Forum
options.description: Open gathering space — +Cultural Influence, ideas spread faster

historicalContext: The Persian Empire created the Royal Road network partly to host ambassadors and messengers. Constantinople's position as a crossroads made it a hub of diplomatic contacts. Infrastructure can be a diplomatic signal as much as a military one.
scaffolding6th: As the Diplomat, I want to build [choice] because [civilization] showed that [fact]. This will affect our relationships with neighboring civilizations by [outcome].
scaffolding78: How does infrastructure communicate political intent to other civilizations? What infrastructure choices signal strength, openness, or ambition?

promptText: Your civilization is older now, and cracks are forming. Some regions want more autonomy. What structure will hold you together?

options.label: Build Regional Governors' Houses
options.description: Grant local power — loyalty increases, some autonomy granted

options.label: Construct a Grand Capital District
options.description: Concentrate power — strong center, peripheral resentment risk

options.label: Create a Federal Council Building
options.description: Shared governance — stable but slower to respond to threats

historicalContext: The Roman Empire struggled with how much autonomy to grant provincial governors. Too little autonomy and regions rebelled. Too much and they declared independence. The British Empire's transition to the Commonwealth explored the same tension centuries later.
scaffolding6th: As the Diplomat, I propose [choice] because growing empires like [civilization] discovered that [fact]. This will help our civilization stay united because [outcome].
scaffolding78: What are the inherent tensions between central authority and regional autonomy? How have different empires managed this tension — successfully and unsuccessfully?

promptText: Your people need a place to remember who they are. The Lorekeeper must decide: what do you build?

options.label: A Sacred Grove or Temple
options.description: Spiritual center — +Cultural Influence, inspires the people

options.label: A Storytelling Hall
options.description: Oral history center — permanent +Legacy per epoch

options.label: A School of Arts
options.description: Train the next generation — doubles CI generation while active

historicalContext: Stonehenge was both a religious site and a cultural anchor for prehistoric Britain. Ancient Greece's Academy shaped Western thought for two millennia. The Timgad library in Roman North Africa preserved knowledge through generations of political change.
scaffolding6th: As the Lorekeeper, I choose to build [choice] because in [civilization], [fact]. Our culture will be strengthened because [outcome].
scaffolding78: How do cultural institutions shape a civilization's identity in ways that physical walls and armies cannot? What cultural buildings have had the most lasting impact in history?

promptText: Your civilization's arts and stories are flourishing. How should you build to preserve and spread them?

options.label: Construct a Great Archive
options.description: Preserve all knowledge — permanent +Legacy, resists Dark Ages

options.label: Build an Artist District
options.description: Concentrated creative output — high CI spread to nearby civilizations

options.label: Establish Festival Grounds
options.description: Public cultural celebrations — +Food morale bonus, +CI each festival

historicalContext: The Library of Alexandria was the ancient world's greatest knowledge repository — its loss during various fires represents one of history's greatest intellectual catastrophes. The Italian Renaissance began in artists' districts concentrated in Florence and Venice.
scaffolding6th: As the Lorekeeper, I want to [choice] because the [example] shows that when civilizations invest in [culture type], they experience [outcome]. Our civilization's culture will [effect].
scaffolding78: What is the relationship between cultural investment and civilizational resilience? How have great archives, artist communities, or festivals shaped the civilizations that created them?

promptText: Your civilization's cultural influence has spread beyond your borders. How will you build to sustain this legacy?

options.label: Build a Grand Museum
options.description: Showcase your civilization's artifacts — massive CI cap increase

options.label: Establish Cultural Missions
options.description: Send Lorekeeper units to spread your culture to other regions

options.label: Fund a University
options.description: Train scholars from other civilizations — shared knowledge, diplomatic bonus

historicalContext: The Smithsonian Institution expanded American cultural influence globally. Al-Azhar University in Cairo, founded in 970 CE, spread Islamic scholarship across the world. Cultural institutions can project power as effectively as armies.
scaffolding6th: As the Lorekeeper, I propose [choice] because our civilization's cultural influence should grow through [reason], just as [historical example] shows that [fact].
scaffolding78: What is 'soft power' and how does cultural influence function as a form of power? Compare a civilization that projected power through culture vs. one that relied on military force.

promptText: Your people are safe for now — but a Warlord knows that preparation is survival. What military infrastructure will you build first?

options.label: Build Barracks
options.description: Train soldiers faster, reduce unit cost by 25%

options.label: Construct Walls
options.description: First attack each epoch absorbed — enemy damage halved

options.label: Build a Watchtower
options.description: Early warning system — see enemy movements 1 epoch in advance

historicalContext: Sparta maintained permanent barracks for its warrior class from childhood called the agoge. The Great Wall of China prioritized fixed defenses. The Byzantine Empire famously used watchtowers connected by signal fires to respond to threats within hours.
scaffolding6th: As the Warlord, I recommend building [choice] because [civilization] proved that [fact]. Our military strength will improve because [outcome].
scaffolding78: What is the difference between an offensive and a defensive military strategy? What historical civilizations best exemplify each approach — and what were the long-term results?

promptText: Tensions with neighbors are rising. As Warlord, you must build the infrastructure that will determine the outcome.

options.label: Build a War Arsenal
options.description: Weapon production facility — unit upgrades cost 30% less

options.label: Establish a Military Academy
options.description: Train elite soldiers — experienced units deal +25% damage

options.label: Build a Naval Yard
options.description: Sea power — blockade capabilities, +Reach in coastal regions

historicalContext: Athens' Piraeus naval yard made it the dominant sea power in Greece. Sparta's military academies produced the ancient world's most feared infantry. Song Dynasty China developed advanced arsenals that pioneered gunpowder weapons centuries before Europe.
scaffolding6th: As the Warlord, I choose to build [choice] because when [civilization] invested in this type of military infrastructure, [fact]. Our forces will benefit by [outcome].
scaffolding78: How does military infrastructure investment affect deterrence vs. escalation? What historical examples show military build-ups that prevented war vs. those that accelerated it?

promptText: Your civilization is powerful, but power must be projected. The Warlord must decide: build for dominance or for control.

options.label: Build a Citadel
options.description: Fortress capital — virtually impossible to conquer your core region

options.label: Construct Border Forts
options.description: Distributed defense at every border sub-zone

options.label: Build a Monument of Victory
options.description: +Cultural Influence among conquered/allied territories — intimidation factor

historicalContext: The Crusader castles in the Holy Land are engineering marvels that controlled critical passages for centuries. Rome's triumphal arches celebrated military victories in ways that projected power through culture. The choice between fortification and intimidation is as old as civilization.
scaffolding6th: As the Warlord, I believe [choice] is the right investment because powerful civilizations like [example] learned that [fact]. This will secure our military position because [outcome].
scaffolding78: What is the purpose of military architecture beyond practical defense? How do fortifications and monuments serve both tactical and psychological roles? Give historical examples of each.

promptText: Your civilization has secured its heartland. As Architect, you must decide how to build into new territory.

options.label: Build a Frontier Settlement
options.description: Establish a new outpost in an unclaimed sub-zone

options.label: Extend your road network
options.description: Connect distant sub-zones — faster expansion in future epochs

options.label: Reinforce borders first
options.description: Strengthen what you have before expanding

historicalContext: American western expansion used railroad infrastructure to make settlement viable. Roman roads preceded permanent settlement of conquered territories. The Vikings established temporary camps that became permanent settlements as infrastructure followed.
scaffolding6th: As the Architect, I think we should [choice] because civilization expansion history shows that [fact]. This will affect our territory growth by [outcome].
scaffolding78: What makes territorial expansion sustainable vs. overextended? What infrastructure is necessary before a civilization can hold new territory?

promptText: You've claimed new land, but settling it is harder than taking it. As Architect, what do you build first in new territory?

options.label: A Supply Depot
options.description: New territory generates resources immediately — no lag period

options.label: A Defensive Wall around the new zone
options.description: New territory has same defense as your heartland

options.label: A Farm or Granary
options.description: Feed the settlers first — new territory grows population faster

historicalContext: Roman coloniae were built to a standard template: roads, forum, walls, and supply depot in a fixed order. The success of the colonial template meant new territory became productive Roman land faster than enemies could reclaim it.
scaffolding6th: As the Architect, I propose building [choice] in our new territory first because [civilization] showed that new settlers need [fact]. Our expansion will succeed because [outcome].
scaffolding78: What is the infrastructure sequence that makes colonization sustainable? Why do some civilizations succeed at integrating new territory while others see their expansions collapse?

promptText: Your empire spans many sub-zones. Managing this territory is becoming a challenge the Architect must solve.

options.label: Build an Imperial Relay Station network
options.description: Fast communication across all territory — response time halved

options.label: Construct Regional Storehouses
options.description: Distribute resource storage — bank decay reduced in all sub-zones

options.label: Build a Grand Canal or Aqueduct system
options.description: Connect water and food across your empire — famine prevention

historicalContext: The Persian Royal Road, stretching 1,600 miles, used relay stations to move messages across the empire in a week. The Roman aqueduct system fed distant cities from mountain sources. Empire-scale infrastructure enabled civilizations to function as a unified whole rather than a patchwork.
scaffolding6th: As the Architect, I recommend [choice] because large empires like [example] discovered that without [infrastructure type], [consequence]. This will help our empire function by [outcome].
scaffolding78: What are the infrastructure challenges unique to large empires that smaller civilizations don't face? How did the Roman or Persian empires engineer solutions to this problem?

promptText: Your explorers have encountered the edges of another civilization's territory. The Diplomat must decide: how do you make first contact?

options.label: Send a peaceful envoy with gifts
options.description: Friendly first contact — opens trade and dialogue options

options.label: Plant your flag and claim the border
options.description: Assert your territory — may cause tension but secures your claim

options.label: Watch and learn before approaching
options.description: Gather intelligence first — better informed when you do make contact

historicalContext: When Columbus arrived in the Caribbean, his approach was aggressive and coercive. The Chinese explorer Zheng He, just decades earlier, made first contact with dozens of civilizations using gifts, trade, and respect — creating diplomatic relationships that lasted for generations.
scaffolding6th: As the Diplomat, I believe we should approach our neighbors by [choice] because [historical example] showed that first contact done through [method] leads to [outcome].
scaffolding78: How does the method of first contact between civilizations shape their long-term relationship? Compare Zheng He's diplomatic approach to European colonial approaches. Why the different strategies?

promptText: Your civilization and a neighbor both want the same unclaimed territory. War could break out. The Diplomat must find another way.

options.label: Propose a shared border treaty
options.description: Divide the disputed zone by agreement — lose half but avoid war

options.label: Request international arbitration
options.description: Bring in a third civilization to decide — lose some pride, gain legitimacy

options.label: Race to settle it first
options.description: Build in it this epoch — finders keepers, but expect resentment

historicalContext: The 1494 Treaty of Tordesillas split the undiscovered world between Spain and Portugal with a line on a map — one of history's first territorial arbitration agreements. The Berlin Conference of 1884 'divided' Africa between European powers, with catastrophic long-term consequences.
scaffolding6th: As the Diplomat, I propose [choice] because disputed territories like [historical example] were handled through [method], leading to [outcome]. Our situation requires [reason].
scaffolding78: What makes a negotiated solution to territorial disputes stable versus unstable over time? Compare successful and unsuccessful territorial treaties in history. What made the difference?

promptText: You have the power to expand — but a smaller civilization sits in your path. Using force would be easy. The Diplomat argues there's a better way.

options.label: Offer a vassal arrangement
options.description: They keep their culture; you get their resources and loyalty

options.label: Propose a union by treaty
options.description: Full integration — their territory becomes yours legally, not by force

options.label: Leave them independent and trade only
options.description: Economic influence without political control

historicalContext: The Roman strategy of offering citizenship to conquered peoples transformed enemies into loyal Roman citizens. The British Empire's system of protectorates maintained some local governance while extracting resources. Some modern states have voluntarily merged with larger neighbors through democratic processes.
scaffolding6th: As the Diplomat, I recommend [choice] because powerful civilizations like [example] discovered that [method of expansion] resulted in [outcome]. This approach is better because [reason].
scaffolding78: What distinguishes conquest, vassalage, union, and economic dominance? What are the long-term costs and benefits of each approach to expansion? Which has historically produced more stable outcomes?

promptText: Your culture remains strong at home — but new lands mean new peoples with their own beliefs. What does the Lorekeeper do?

options.label: Send cultural missionaries
options.description: Spread your stories and beliefs into new territories — +CI spread

options.label: Document the new culture you find
options.description: Record and preserve what you discover — +Legacy, +Reach

options.label: Create a cultural exchange center
options.description: Create a place where cultures blend — shared CI benefits both civs

historicalContext: Spanish missionaries followed conquistadors into the Americas, aggressively converting indigenous populations. Muslim scholars along the Silk Road documented the cultures they encountered. The exchanges at the Tang Dynasty court in Chang'an gave China access to music, art, and ideas from across Eurasia.
scaffolding6th: As the Lorekeeper, I think we should [choice] because when cultures expand, [historical example] shows that [fact]. Our civilization's cultural identity will be [outcome].
scaffolding78: What is cultural imperialism, and how does it differ from cultural exchange? Give historical examples of each. What responsibilities do more powerful civilizations have when they encounter smaller ones?

promptText: As your territory grows, your mythology needs to grow with it. New lands come with new stories. How does the Lorekeeper handle this?

options.label: Create a founding myth for the new land
options.description: New territory gets its own cultural origin story — +CI in new zones

options.label: Incorporate local legends into your mythology
options.description: Blend new stories with yours — +Cultural Influence with former locals

options.label: Commission a Grand Epic poem or saga
options.description: Epic story that unites all territories under one cultural identity — huge +CI

historicalContext: The Aeneid was commissioned by Augustus Caesar to tie Roman identity to the Trojan War, legitimizing Rome's expansion across the Mediterranean. Indigenous myths were often incorporated into colonial religious systems to ease cultural conversion across history.
scaffolding6th: As the Lorekeeper, I propose [choice] because when [civilization] expanded, their cultural strategy was to [fact], which led to [outcome]. Our stories should [approach].
scaffolding78: How do civilizations use narrative and mythology to consolidate control over new territories? Is cultural expansion more or less effective than military expansion in the long run?

promptText: Your civilization's culture spans many territories. But distance strains cultural unity. The Lorekeeper must act to hold it together.

options.label: Establish a central cultural calendar
options.description: Shared festivals across all territories — CI unity bonus, population morale

options.label: Create regional cultural traditions
options.description: Each territory gets its own variation — less CI but more local loyalty

options.label: Fund traveling cultural troupes
options.description: Live performance spreads culture person-to-person — slow but deep CI penetration

historicalContext: Rome created shared holidays (like Saturnalia) that were celebrated from Britain to Mesopotamia. The Islamic Hajj pilgrimage unified Muslims across continents around a shared cultural moment. Medieval European carnival traditions adapted locally while maintaining recognizable shared elements.
scaffolding6th: As the Lorekeeper, I recommend [choice] because large empires like [example] kept their cultures unified through [fact]. Our civilization will stay culturally connected because [outcome].
scaffolding78: What holds a geographically dispersed civilization's culture together? What evidence do we have for how Roman, Islamic, or Chinese cultures maintained cohesion across vast distances?

promptText: Your scouts have found valuable land. Some of it is unclaimed. Some is contested. The Warlord must advise: how far do we push?

options.label: Take only clearly unclaimed territory
options.description: Cautious expansion — no conflict risk, fewer zones

options.label: Push to the most defensible natural border
options.description: Strategic depth — secure the best terrain, expect contestation

options.label: Maximum expansion now while we're strong
options.description: Aggressive claim — maximum territory but high war risk

historicalContext: Alexander the Great's expansions outpaced his supply lines, leading to overextension. Augustus Caesar halted Roman expansion at the Rhine and Danube, declaring these natural borders sufficient — and Rome was stronger for it. The decision of when to stop expanding is often the most important military decision a civilization makes.
scaffolding6th: As the Warlord, I advise [choice] because military history shows that [civilization] learned the hard way that [fact]. Expanding [how far] will lead to [outcome].

promptText: A neighbor's territory sits between yours and a rich resource zone. The Warlord sees an opportunity. But the Diplomat urges caution.

options.label: Negotiate a military corridor

options.description: Avoid the conflict — costs extra Resilience but preserves peace

options.label: Demand the right of passage
options.description: Show of force — may work if they fear you, may trigger war

promptText: You have pushed your borders to their current limits. Now the question is: hold them or continue?

options.description: Locked borders — expensive but secure against all threats

options.description: Push threats further away through diplomacy and trade

promptText: Your civilization's original laws no longer fit the complex society you've built. The Diplomat must shepherd a constitutional moment.

promptText: The way your city looks tells the world who you are. As Architect in the DEFINE round, what cultural statement will you make in stone?

promptText: Your civilization's cultural achievements should be commemorated. As Architect, you must build the monument that defines your legacy.

promptText: Your civilization needs economic laws. As Merchant in the DEFINE round, you have the floor. What economic system will you enshrine?

options.label: Free market law — trade without restriction
options.description: +Reach each epoch, some inequality risk

options.label: Regulated trade law — fair prices enforced
options.description: Stable prices, +Food morale, slightly lower Reach gains

options.label: State monopoly on key goods

historicalContext: Medieval European guilds created regulated trade that protected quality but also created monopolies. The East India Company was essentially a state-backed monopoly that became more powerful than governments. Ancient Rome tried both — markets for grain, state control for salt. Each system produced different social effects.

promptText: Trade between your civilization and others has grown complex. The Merchant must define the rules of international commerce.

promptText: Peacetime requires military law. As Warlord in the DEFINE round, you must define the rules that govern your warriors.

Rome's legions were professional soldiers supported by citizen-farmer reserves — a balance that lasted centuries. Japan's samurai code (Bushido) created highly effective warriors constrained by honor rules.

promptText: After recent conflict, your civilization must decide: what are the laws of war? The Warlord has the floor.

Sun Tzu wrote that treating captured soldiers well and releasing them converted them into assets. The laws of war have consistently proven that restraint, paradoxically, is often strategically effective.

promptText: A long-term threat has emerged. The Architect must design infrastructure that will defend your civilization for generations.

The Byzantine Empire's Theodosian Walls surrounding Constantinople withstood 23 sieges over a thousand years until artillery made them obsolete. Good defensive design accounts for the technology of future threats, not just current ones.

promptText: A threat emerges. Before a single soldier moves, the Diplomat has a job to do in the DEFEND round. What's your move?
